6 min read
Just now

--

Enterprise AI is learning how to govern runtime intent. It still assumes authored business intent already exists.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Author Intent First. Govern Execution Second.Something interesting is happening in enterprise AI.
The word intent is suddenly everywhere.
Agent governance platforms describe intent precedence models. Architecture papers discuss intent layers as a foundational primitive for autonomous systems. Control planes promise to keep AI behavior aligned with organizational intent. Vendors increasingly position intent as the key to making AI trustworthy at enterprise scale.
This is genuinely encouraging.
After years of treating AI adoption as primarily a model problem, a data problem, or a technology problem, the industry is converging on a deeper realization: autonomous systems are only as trustworthy as the intent that governs them.
But within that convergence, an important distinction is being lost.
Two very different things are now being called intent.
One defines what the enterprise wants to remain true.
The other ensures technology behaves consistently with that definition.
The industry increasingly talks about the second while often assuming the first already exists.
One is runtime intent.
The other is authored business intent.
They are not the same thing. They are not created by the same people. They do not exist at the same layer of the enterprise. And confusing them can be a costly mistake organizations make as AI becomes embedded in business operations.
The Enterprise AI Industry Has Discovered Runtime Intent
Most of today’s AI governance conversation focuses on runtime intent.
Runtime intent is the set of controls that determine how an AI system behaves once it is operating.
It includes:

• Policies that constrain what an agent may do

• Permissions that determine what systems it may access

• Guardrails that prevent prohibited actions

• Escalation paths for exceptions

• Monitoring that detects deviation

• Precedence rules that resolve conflicting instructions

• Enforcement mechanisms that ensure compliance
This work is essential.
As enterprises deploy agents to recommend, prioritize, automate, negotiate, optimize, and execute, runtime governance becomes critical infrastructure.
Without it, organizations risk unpredictable behavior, operational inconsistency, and loss of control.
But notice what runtime intent actually does.
It answers the question:
Is the system behaving according to the instructions it has been given?
That is a valuable question.
It is not the first question.
To be clear, modern AI control planes are solving an important problem. Intent models, policy engines, guardrails, and orchestration layers are becoming essential enterprise infrastructure. The issue is not that these platforms lack governance. The issue is that governance presumes something exists to govern.
The Question Runtime Governance Cannot Answer
Every runtime governance platform assumes something important already exists.
It assumes the enterprise knows what it wants.
It assumes someone has already defined the outcomes that matter, the boundaries that cannot be crossed, the tradeoffs that are acceptable, the evidence that proves success, and the accountability model that governs decisions.
In other words, it assumes intent has already been authored.
But where does that authored business intent come from?
Much of the enterprise AI conversation quietly skips over this question.
The control plane cannot determine what a business should optimize.
A guardrail cannot decide what level of risk is acceptable.
An agent framework cannot determine who should own a decision.
Runtime governance can enforce decisions.
It cannot originate them.
What Authored Business Intent Actually Is
Authored business intent is the deliberate expression of how an enterprise intends to operate.
It is created before implementation begins.
It defines:

• Desired business outcomes

• Conditions of success

• Decision boundaries

• Accountability structures

• Control requirements

• Evidence requirements

• Escalation expectations

• Risk tolerances

• Optimization priorities

• Acceptable tradeoffs
Authored business intent is not configuration.
It is not policy syntax.
It is not a permissions matrix.
It is not a workflow definition.
It is the business operating logic that all of those things are supposed to reflect.
Most importantly, authored business intent is a leadership responsibility.
It is business-side work.
The people closest to strategy, accountability, governance, and outcomes must decide what must remain true before technology begins translating those decisions into implementations.
Enforcement Cannot Create Intent
The relationship between authored business intent and runtime intent is straightforward.
Authored business intent determines what should be true.
Runtime intent ensures execution remains aligned with it.
The problem arises when organizations attempt to start with the second layer while assuming the first already exists.
When authored business intent is absent, runtime governance does not stop functioning.
It simply enforces something else.
That “something else” often becomes:

• Vendor assumptions

• Industry templates

• Legacy processes

• Consultant interpretations

• Workshop conclusions

• Requirements written under schedule pressure

• Configuration defaults
The governance layer faithfully enforces whatever it receives.
The challenge is that what it receives may not represent a conscious business decision.
The enforcement may be flawless.
The intent may be accidental.
As AI governance technology becomes more sophisticated, this risk increases rather than decreases.
A highly capable control plane can consistently enforce operational choices the organization never deliberately made.
The Hidden Risk: Automating Drift
Many organizations believe governance prevents drift.
But governance can also institutionalize drift.
If business intent was never explicitly authored, implementation teams inevitably fill the gaps. They make reasonable choices. They interpret ambiguous requirements. They resolve conflicts. They create operating assumptions.
Over time those assumptions become embedded in systems, workflows, automations, and now AI agents.
Eventually everyone forgets which decisions were explicit and which were inferred.
The organization begins treating implementation artifacts as if they were business intent.
A governance platform then arrives and does exactly what it was designed to do.
It enforces those embedded assumptions consistently.
What appears to be governance may actually be the automation of accumulated interpretation.
This is not a technology problem.
It is an authorship problem.
Beyond “Human in the Loop”
Enterprise AI discussions increasingly focus on keeping humans in the loop or above the loop.
Those concepts matter.
Human oversight remains essential.
But supervision is not the same as governance.
A human reviewing outputs is participating after execution has already begun.
The more important decisions occurred earlier.
Someone decided:

• What the AI should optimize

• Which objectives have priority

• Which exceptions are acceptable

• Which controls are mandatory

• What evidence constitutes success

• Who owns the outcome
If these decisions were never made explicitly, a human above the loop is not governing.
They are observing.
The stronger model is different.
The stronger position is that humans govern the loop.
Leadership authors intent before execution begins, and every downstream layer exists to execute, enforce, validate, and evidence that intent.
Runtime governance becomes a mechanism of alignment rather than a substitute for decision-making.
Two Layers, Two Disciplines
The enterprise ultimately needs both forms of intent.
It needs runtime intent because autonomous systems require governance.
And it needs authored business intent because governance requires something worth governing.
These are complementary disciplines.
One focuses on operational enforcement.
The other focuses on business authorship.
One determines how systems behave.
The other determines what the business expects those systems to achieve.
One is largely being built by AI platform providers, governance vendors, architects, and control-plane designers.
The other remains largely unaddressed.
The industry is investing heavily in governing execution while spending far less time defining what execution should actually govern.
Organizations still lack structured methods for translating executive priorities into explicit, governable operating intent before implementation begins.
Yet every runtime governance platform assumes that work has already been completed.
The Question Before Governance
Before asking how your AI will be governed, ask a more fundamental question.
Has your organization explicitly authored the intent that governance is expected to enforce?
Can leadership clearly articulate:

• The outcomes that matter

• The conditions of success

• The decision boundaries that must be respected

• The tradeoffs that are acceptable

• The evidence required to prove alignment

• The accountability model behind every critical decision
If the answer depends on tribal knowledge, workshop notes, requirements documents, or assumptions embedded inside implementations, then the problem is not governance.
The problem is missing authorship.
Runtime governance is becoming one of the most important capabilities in enterprise AI.
But no governance layer, no matter how sophisticated, can compensate for intent that was never deliberately created.
Before enterprises govern AI behavior, they must author the business intent that behavior is meant to serve.
Only then can governance enforce alignment rather than simply enforcing drift.
The future of enterprise AI will require increasingly sophisticated runtime governance.
But runtime governance is downstream.
Before an enterprise can govern how AI behaves, it must define what the business intends.
Otherwise the organization risks building extraordinarily capable systems that faithfully enforce decisions it never consciously made.
Author business intent before implementation begins.
